8 min read
1 hour ago

--

What one-shot generation gets wrong, why asynchronous AI workflows are harder than they look, and what I learned while building WriteGeniuses.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I started building WriteGeniuses, the first version followed the familiar AI writing pattern:

• Collect a title and a few keywords.

• Build one large prompt.

• Ask the model to generate an article.

• Save the response as Markdown.
It worked well enough for a demo.
It did not work well enough for a product.
The longer the requested article became, the more obvious the problems were. Sections repeated each other. Introductions were too long. Important points disappeared halfway through the response. Sometimes the final paragraph ended in the middle of a sentence.
The biggest problem was not generation quality, though. It was control.
If one section was weak, the user could not fix that section without regenerating the whole article. A second generation might improve the weak section while making three good sections worse.
That pushed me toward a different architecture: treat content generation as a workflow made of plans, units, and asynchronous jobs.
The Problem with One-Shot Article Generation
Generating an entire article with one model request looks attractive because the implementation is simple.
You send something like this:
Write a 2,500-word article about the requested topic.Include an introduction, six sections, a conclusion, and FAQs.
Use the primary keyword naturally.
Return Markdown.The model returns one large block of text, and the job appears to be finished.
In practice, several responsibilities are mixed into that single request:

• Understanding the user’s intent

• Deciding the search intent

• Designing the article structure

• Allocating the word count

• Choosing what evidence is needed

• Writing every section

• Maintaining consistency

• Formatting the final Markdown

• Staying within the output limit
When the response is poor, it is difficult to identify which stage failed.
Was the article outline weak? Was the research insufficient? Did the model misunderstand the audience? Did it simply run out of output tokens?
The user only sees the final result.
There is another practical issue: large responses are fragile. A model can produce valid Markdown for several thousand tokens and still stop halfway through the final section. Asking for structured JSON around the entire article adds another failure mode because one missing quote or closing brace can invalidate the complete response.
I wanted the system to fail in smaller, recoverable pieces.
Splitting the Workflow into Brief, Plan, and Units
WriteGeniuses now treats an article as three separate layers.
1. The Brief
The brief contains the user’s intent:

• Topic

• Primary keyword

• Audience

• Search intent

• Tone

• Content angle

• Target length

• Research requirements

• Optional notes and constraints
The brief should describe what the user wants without pretending to know the final structure.
2. The Plan
The plan converts that intent into an executable content specification.
For a blog article, it can describe:

• Metadata and suggested title

• Context and scope

• Paragraph order

• The purpose of each section

• Points each section must cover

• Supporting keywords

• Research references

• Required tables or diagrams

• FAQ questions

• Visual recommendations
The plan is not displayed as the finished article. It is closer to an editorial blueprint.
This separation matters because planning and writing are different tasks. A good writer can produce polished prose from a weak outline, but the result will still be poorly organized.
By validating the plan first, the system can catch structural problems before spending time and tokens on every section.
3. The Units
Each paragraph or FAQ becomes an independent unit.
A unit has its own:

• Identifier

• Type

• Position

• Heading or question

• Purpose

• Required coverage

• References

• Generation status

• Markdown output
Instead of asking Gemini to write the whole article, WriteGeniuses asks it to write individual units according to the approved plan.
This provides a much better editing model. Users can edit one paragraph, retry one failed FAQ, or regenerate one section without touching the rest of the article.
Failure becomes local instead of global.
Why the First Convex Workflow Struggled
WriteGeniuses uses Convex for data, authentication, real-time updates, and backend functions.
The original generation workflow called Gemini from Convex actions and waited for each response. The workflow was conceptually straightforward:
Create article
-> Generate plan
-> Create units
-> Generate units
-> Save Markdown
-> Mark article completeThis worked during early testing, especially when only one article was running.
The problems appeared when requests became slower or several units were generated concurrently.
A model request could remain open for minutes. During that time, the Convex action was still active. If enough requests waited at once, workers became unavailable for unrelated functions.
I started seeing errors such as:
There are no available workers to process the requestSome actions also reached the maximum execution duration. Retrying inside the same action made the situation worse because the worker remained occupied while waiting for another external response.
The application needed concurrency, but increasing concurrency meant increasing the number of long-running workers.
That was the wrong tradeoff.
The backend should coordinate generation. It should not spend most of its time waiting for the model.
Moving Generation to Gemini Batch API
The next version moved Gemini requests to the Batch API.
Instead of waiting for content inside a Convex action, the backend now:

• Creates a local batch job record.

• Creates one or more batch request records.

• Submits the requests to Gemini.

• Stores the returned Google batch name.

• Ends the Convex action.
The worker is released as soon as the job is submitted.
Gemini processes the requests asynchronously. When the batch finishes, a static webhook calls a Convex HTTP action. The webhook does very little work itself:

• Verify the webhook signature.

• Extract the Gemini batch name.

• Find the corresponding local job.

• Schedule internal processing.

• Return a successful response quickly.
An internal action then retrieves the batch result, validates each response, saves successful units, records failures, and advances the article to the next stage.
The generation chain now looks like this:
Brief
-> Plan batch
-> Webhook
-> Validate and save plan
-> Unit batch
-> Webhook
-> Validate and save units
-> Optional image recommendation batch
-> Final articleThis design adds more states and database records, but it removes the need to keep backend workers alive while Gemini is generating content.
Mapping Batch Responses Back to Units
Batch processing creates a new question: how do you know which response belongs to which unit?
I initially considered asking the model to return the unit ID:
{
"unitId": "p_introduction",
"markdown": "..."
}That looks reasonable, but it gives the model responsibility for information the application already knows.
The model can omit the ID, change it, or return the wrong one. There is no benefit in making that association probabilistic.
WriteGeniuses now creates a requestKey for every batch request. The request record stores the target article, stage, unit ID, order, status, and retry information.
When a response arrives, its batch request identity is mapped back to that stored record. The content payload only needs to contain generated content.
The application owns identity. The model only writes text.
Blog Articles and X Threads Need Different Plans
After the blog workflow was working, I added support for X threads.
My first attempt reused too much of the article architecture.
Both formats contain ordered pieces of text, so it was tempting to treat an X post as a short paragraph. That produced technically valid but awkward results.
Posts often sounded like article fragments:
Bilibili has become an important platform for...Some started in the middle of an idea. Others ended with model notes, character-count checks, or incomplete sentences.
The problem was not only the unit prompt. The plan itself still thought like a blog article.
A blog section usually develops one subject over several paragraphs. An X post has a different job. It may need to create a hook, establish context, deliver one insight, show an example, or close the thread.
I introduced a planKind field and separated the two content systems.
A blog article uses:

• Blog-specific brief fields

• A blog plan schema

• Paragraph and FAQ units

• Markdown-oriented generation rules

• Optional visual recommendations
An X thread uses:

• An objective

• A point of view

• A target audience

• A desired post count

• Thread-specific examples or source material

• Post roles such as hook, context, insight, example, and CTA

• A character limit for each unit
Each format now has its own plan prompt, schema, unit prompt, validator, preview, and export format.
The shared workflow coordinates jobs and storage, but it does not force every type of writing into the same content model.
That boundary will matter when more formats are added.
Structured Output Is Helpful, Not Magical
Gemini supports structured JSON output through responseMimeType and responseSchema.
That makes AI responses more predictable, but it does not make them infallible.
During development, I encountered several different failures:

• JSON that stopped before the closing brace

• A batch job that succeeded while an individual request failed

• Valid JSON that did not satisfy the final Zod schema

• Empty structured responses

• Schemas that were too complex for the model constraint system

• Different behavior between responseSchema and responseJsonSchema
One particularly confusing error was:
Constraint is too tallThe problem was not the prompt length. The generated constraint used to enforce the output schema had become too complex.
The fix was not to build increasingly clever JSON repair logic. I had already removed that path because silently repairing model output makes behavior difficult to reason about.
Instead, I simplified the schemas and separated them by content type.
The current pattern is:

• Send a focused Gemini responseSchema.

• Request application/json.

• Parse the returned JSON.

• Validate it again with Zod.

• Mark the individual request as failed if validation does not pass.

• Retry only the affected stage or unit.
The model schema guides generation. Zod remains the final trust boundary.
Mermaid Found Another Edge Case
WriteGeniuses can generate Markdown containing Mermaid diagrams.
A valid-looking diagram such as this caused a parser error:
graph TD
A[Developer Application] --> B{Creem API Gateway}
B -- Requires --> C{Authentication (API Keys)}The parentheses inside a node label and the older edge-label syntax were not consistently parsed.
The generation prompt now requires two rules:

• Wrap every node label in double quotes.

• Write labeled edges as -->|Label|.
The safer version is:
graph TD
A["Developer Application"] --> B{"Creem API Gateway"}
B -->|Requires| C{"Authentication (API Keys)"}This was a useful reminder that “valid Markdown” is not a single standard. Generated content must also satisfy the parser used by the product.
The Current Architecture
The frontend now uses TanStack Start and TanStack Router.
Protected pages fetch initial data through route loaders so the server-rendered HTML contains useful content. After hydration, Convex subscriptions take over and provide real-time updates.
The backend handles:

• Authentication

• Articles and content units

• Templates

• Batch jobs and requests

• Webhook processing

• Retry state

• User credits

• Payment events
Gemini handles generation, while Serp
